$generated: Hand-translated English bundle for the home-page lísteček (clover-fact card). Keys are the numeric `id` values from clover-texts.shuffled.json. The shuffled CS file remains the canonical source — this file only carries title/text (and kind for author entries) per id, looked up at render time when locale === 'en'. Author entries' personal voice was translated as faithfully as the medium allows; some Czech-specific cultural references are kept in the EN body so the original meaning isn't lost.

1.title: White clover
1.text: The classic four-leaf clover most often comes from white clover (Trifolium repens), not red clover (Trifolium pratense) as many believe. White clover is a low, creeping plant with white flowers that grows almost everywhere — on lawns, field margins and in parks.

2.title: Genetic anomaly
2.text: The four-leaf clover arises from a rare genetic mutation. Researchers have identified a recessive gene responsible for the deviation, but its expression also depends on environmental conditions — temperature, humidity and soil nutrients.

3.title: Probability of finding one
3.text: The often-quoted ratio is 1:10,000, but a large 2017 study (5.7 million clovers across six European countries) found the actual ratio is closer to 1:5,076. A five-leaf clover occurs at roughly 1:24,390 and a six-leaf at 1:312,500.

4.title: Celtic tradition
4.text: The druids believed the four-leaf clover bestowed the ability to see evil spirits and protected against black magic. They picked it with the left hand to preserve its magic. The trefoil symbolised earth, sea and sky — the fourth leaf added the spiritual realm.

5.title: Eve in paradise
5.text: Christian legend says Eve took a four-leaf clover with her on her exile from paradise as a memento of Eden. That is why finding one is considered a reminder of the lost paradise — a small piece of the happiness humanity left behind.

6.title: Symbol of four gifts
6.text: Traditionally the four leaves stand for four gifts: faith, hope, love and luck. Another common variant lists glory, wealth, love and health. The fifth leaf adds money, the sixth fame, the seventh longevity.

7.title: The genus Trifolium
7.text: The Latin genus name Trifolium comes from tri (three) and folium (leaf) — literally “trefoil”. The four-leaf clover is a rare developmental anomaly of common clover (most often white clover, Trifolium repens): a palmately compound leaf with one leaflet more than the usual three. It is not a separate species but a mutation occurring at a ratio of roughly 1 : 10,000. (Note: the Czech “jetel zvrhlý” is, by contrast, a separate species — Alsike clover, Trifolium hybridum — unrelated to the four-leaf clover.)

8.title: World record
8.text: The largest known number of leaflets on a single stem is 63. The record is held by Yoshiharu Watanabe of Nasushiobara, Japan, who discovered it on 2 August 2023. The previous record of 56 leaflets was held by Shigeo Obara of Hanamaki since 2009.

9.title: To see a unicorn
9.text: A medieval superstition held that anyone carrying a four-leaf clover could see unicorns, fairies and other hidden creatures of nature. The leaf was also said to reveal hidden treasures and entrances to other worlds.

10.title: Irish symbol
10.text: Careful — the Irish national symbol is the shamrock (a three-leaf), not the four-leaf clover. According to legend, Saint Patrick used the trefoil to explain the Trinity. The four-leaf is a more general symbol of luck that became associated with Ireland only secondarily.

11.title: Tetraploid mutation
11.text: Some clover lines are tetraploid — they carry double the chromosomes. Four-leaf forms appear more often in these plants than in the regular diploid type. Breeders use this trait to cultivate ornamental varieties.

12.title: Growing anticlockwise
12.text: A four-leaf clover is a rare developmental deviation of the trefoil (genus Trifolium). Its leaflets grow palmately from a single common point and the position of the “fourth” leaflet is not fixed — botany knows no rule that it grows anticlockwise. If a direction is to be described in compound leaves, it is the order in which leaflet primordia are laid down along the leaf axis (acropetal or basipetal), not any rotation around a centre.

13.title: Medieval knights
13.text: Medieval knights would sew a four-leaf clover into the lining of their armour before battle. They believed it would protect them from injury and ensure their safe return home. Some family coats of arms from that era still bear the four-leaf today.

14.title: Leaf of luck
14.text: “When the quiet wind ripples through the grass, you’ll find on the meadow four little leaves — a dream sleeps in them, luck plays in them, just bend down and don’t lose your peace.” — anonymous Moravian folk rhyme

15.title: Karel Čapek
15.text: In The Gardener's Year, Karel Čapek calls the four-leaf clover “a plant we look for without believing we will find it — and that is precisely why we sometimes do”. For many collectors this line is a guiding leitmotif.

16.title: The Čtyřlístek comic
16.text: In Bohemia the best-known comic is probably Čtyřlístek (“Four-Leaf Clover”) by Jaroslav Němeček, published since 15 May 1969. The four friends are the dog Fifinka, the rabbit Pinďa, the piglet Bobík and the cat Myšpulín — each with a distinct character: Myšpulín the brilliant inventor, Bobík the strongman and big eater, Fifinka the tidy homemaker and cook, and Pinďa the timid sportsman.

17.title: Trifolium pratense
17.text: Red clover (Trifolium pratense) has pinkish-violet flowers and grows upright. Its leaves are larger and usually carry a white V-shaped mark. Four-leaf forms occur on it too, but less often than on white clover.

18.title: The record collection
18.text: The record for the largest clover collection was long held by American Edward Martin of Cooper Landing, Alaska — in 2007 he had 111,060 four-leaf clovers according to Guinness World Records (newspaper sources from 2008 cite up to around 160,000). He pressed his finds into books. Since 2023 the record has been held by Gabriella Gerhardt of Wisconsin with 118,791 four-leaf clovers.

19.title: Adam and Eve in the garden
19.text: Some Central European tales say the four-leaf clover is the only piece of paradise that Adam and Eve carried with them to earth. To find one is to glimpse the time before the Fall.

20.title: Under the pillow
20.text: Czech folk tradition advises placing a found four-leaf clover under the pillow at night — your future love is then said to appear in your dream. If a girl keeps a four-leaf under her pillow, she will dream of the person she will marry.

21.title: The clover genome
21.text: The genome of white clover (Trifolium repens) was sequenced to chromosome level with haplotype resolution in 2023 (the team of James Santangelo at the University of Toronto Mississauga, in the journal Genome Biology and Evolution); a separate chromosome-scale assembly by another team appeared the same year. White clover is an allotetraploid — it arose by hybridisation of two diploid ancestors, T. occidentale and T. pallescens — and this assembly annotated 78,174 protein-coding genes.

22.title: Five-leaf clover
22.text: A five-leaf clover is even rarer — roughly 1:24,390 according to the 2017 study. By tradition it brings money or financial success. A six-leaf (1:312,500) symbolises fame, a seven-leaf a long life.

23.title: Queen Victoria
23.text: Britain's Queen Victoria was a known collector of four-leaf clovers. She kept a special album in which she pressed them with the date and place of finding. The album was discovered in the Buckingham Palace archives among her belongings.

24.title: Fragment from a Carafe
24.text: “In my palm a tiny four-leaf, a raindrop sparkling on glass — what is greater in it, luck or a dream? — who knows, who knows, who knows.” — fragment of a folk song from the Slovácko region

25.title: Hare's-foot clover
25.text: Trifolium arvense, hare's-foot clover (colloquially “cat clover” or “hare's tails”), is a strikingly hairy species. Four-leaf clovers occur on it more rarely than on T. repens, but its soft, furry, cylindrical flower heads are unmistakable.

26.title: NBC Peacock
26.text: The 1956 logo of the American TV network NBC depicted a stylised peacock with eleven brightly coloured feathers, meant to promote colour broadcasting and the sale of colour television sets. It was created by John J. Graham (with designer Herb Lubalin). It has no connection to the four-leaf clover.

27.title: Roman Saturn
27.text: In Roman mythology the clover was dedicated to Saturn, patron of agriculture and wealth. The four-leaf form was considered his particular gift — whoever found one was thought to be chosen.

28.title: 21 leaflets
28.text: Before Watanabe's 2023 record (63 leaflets), the world record was held by Shigeo Obara — 56 leaflets found in 2009 in Hanamaki. Obara reached this number through decades of natural cross-breeding of plants prone to extra leaflets.

29.title: Above the door
29.text: Scots believed that a four-leaf clover hung above the doorway would draw luck into the house, but if it fell, it meant luck was leaving. The leaf was therefore often slipped into a picture frame above the door.

30.title: Jaroslav Vrchlický
30.text: Jaroslav Vrchlický is among the most prolific Czech poets; in 1889 he published the melancholy collection Hořká jádra (“Bitter Kernels”), which reflected his creative crisis and disillusion. The specific poem “Čtyřlístek štěstí” and a dating to 1882, however, do not appear anywhere in his documented work.

31.title: The role of environment
31.text: The formation of a four-leaf clover is influenced by both genetics and environment. A 2010 genetic study (Crop Science, University of Georgia) identified a gene responsible for the fourth leaflet and found that its expression depends strongly on environmental conditions — especially light and temperature. That mild stress (drought, trampling, pollution) would directly raise the frequency of four-leaf clovers was not, however, shown by this or any other study; it is rather a widespread assumption science has yet to confirm.

32.title: Earliest mention
32.text: The earliest written mention of the four-leaf clover as a symbol of luck dates to 1620, in the English book Astrologaster by John Melton. Melton wrote that anyone who finds a four-leaf plant on a walk through the fields will soon find something good as well.

33.title: First mention
33.text: The earliest surviving written mention of the four-leaf clover as a symbol of luck dates to 1620, from the English book The Good Husband's Jewel. The author John Melton advises how a husbandman might find a four-leaf clover on his land.

34.title: Leaf and night
34.text: “Luck is like a four-leaf clover — you don’t look for it, it finds you. And when it finds you, don’t ask why you — bend down, take it, and walk on.” — Vladimír Holan, fragment from his diaries

35.title: Symbiotic bacteria
35.text: Clover belongs to the legume family and has root nodules with bacteria of the genus Rhizobium. These bacteria fix atmospheric nitrogen and enrich the soil with it. That is why clover is grown as green manure and a soil regenerator.

36.title: Music album
36.text: In 2008 the band Coldplay released a special edition of their album Viva la Vida that contained a real pressed four-leaf clover as a fan bonus. It was a limited run of 5,000 copies and is now a collector's rarity.

37.title: Norse tradition
37.text: The Vikings believed the four-leaf clover was given to Loki by the goddess Freyja as a token of reconciliation after one of his pranks. The bearer of a four-leaf was said to enjoy Loki's protection — and his caprice.

38.title: Collector Kosina
38.text: The Czech record-holder for collecting four-leaf clovers is Jaroslav Kosina of Mikulčice, who has collected since 1996 and has gathered over 20,000 four-leaf clovers. He presses undamaged specimens into a herbarium; his collection was exhibited at the Museum of Records and Curiosities in Pelhřimov and is entered in the Czech Book of Records.

39.title: Spinning leaves
39.text: In Slovácko it was said that twirling a four-leaf clover between the fingers clockwise would summon love. Counter-clockwise, it would drive away an evil curse. Three twirls and a kiss completed the ritual.

40.title: Hans Christian Andersen
40.text: There is no four-leaf clover in Andersen's fairy tale The Snow Queen — Gerda finds Kai and returns home thanks to a chain of helpers (the river, a crow, a prince and princess, a robber girl, a reindeer, a Lapp woman and a Finn woman) and the power of her love, not thanks to a clover.

41.title: Apomixis
41.text: Clover does not reproduce apomictically — apomixis (seed formation without fertilisation) has not been documented in legumes (Fabaceae), including Trifolium, and white clover (Trifolium repens) is, thanks to gametophytic self-incompatibility, almost exclusively cross-pollinated, so it needs insect pollination to set seed. A higher local frequency of four-leaf clovers is therefore usually not caused by asexual reproduction but by vegetative clonal spread of a single mutant plant via runners (stolons), together with the shared genetics and environment of that colony.

42.title: Search instinct
42.text: People who find one four-leaf clover are statistically more likely to find another on the same day. Scientists call this a “search image” — the brain tunes itself to a specific shape and starts actively looking for it.

43.title: Napoleon's talisman
43.text: A story circulated that Napoleon Bonaparte carried a four-leaf clover at the battle of Marengo (1800) and that it was the reason he won. Historians never confirmed the legend, but it became part of the 19th-century Napoleonic cult.

44.title: The way home
44.text: “Don't seek your luck in distant lands; it blooms at your feet — bend down and find the four-leaf clover that has always been here.” — Otokar Březina, paraphrase from the collection Builders of the Temple

45.title: Lifespan
45.text: An individual white-clover plant usually lives 2–3 years, but it spreads vegetatively through creeping stolons and forms clonal colonies that can persist for decades. A four-leaf you find today may belong to the very same plant as last year's.

46.title: Volkswagen Beetle
46.text: The four-leaf clover was a popular motif on motorcycles and cars in the 1960s — the VW Beetle with a four-leaf-clover sticker on the rear window was famous. The sticker was meant to protect the driver and passengers from accidents.

47.title: The Egyptian goddess
47.text: Some readings of Egyptian hieroglyphs link the goddess Isis with a four-leaf plant that grew along the Nile. It symbolised the four basic elements and Isis's ability to control nature.

48.title: Youngest finder
48.text: Even a small child can stumble upon a four-leaf clover by chance, and many collectors remember their very first find from childhood. But there is no official “youngest finder” category — the real records (Guinness) track only the number of clovers collected or the number of leaflets on a single stem, never the finder's age.

49.title: Lunchtime clover
49.text: In eastern Bohemia there was a custom that whoever finds a four-leaf clover before lunch will have a happy day. Found in the afternoon, the luck goes to someone in the family. Found at night, it foretells a change of fate.

50.title: James Joyce
50.text: In Joyce's Ulysses a four-leaf clover appears only once — in a parodic passage of the Cyclops episode as a “four-leaved shamrock” (emeralds on an engagement ring). The word “clover” otherwise falls just twice, only in the idiom “in clover” (living in comfort). It is neither a leitmotif nor a symbol of Bloom's search for himself, and Bloom finds no four-leaf clover.

51.title: Frequency in the population
51.text: The frequency of four-leaf clovers is not set by a simple genetic ratio. The tendency to form a fourth leaflet is a recessive trait in white clover (usually requiring matching alleles from both parents, in its tetraploid genome) and is strongly influenced by the environment. In the wild a four-leaf clover therefore occurs in roughly 1 in 5,000 to 1 in 10,000 plants — a large 2017 European survey (~5.7 million clovers) gives about 1 : 5,076.

52.title: Symmetry
52.text: A genuine four-leaf clover has its leaflets arranged symmetrically into a square. False four-leaf clovers (cultivated, glued) are often asymmetric or have leaflets of an atypical shape. Collectors can spot the difference at a glance.

53.title: Soko castle
53.text: At Soko castle in Poland a 14th-century manuscript was found in 1923 with precise instructions for hunting four-leaf clovers. Its author was a monk Jakub of Olomouc, who recommended morning dew and singing while searching.

54.title: Summer field
54.text: “Clover, clover, blooming white, four leaves at once — bend down and you have luck, walk past and you'll oversleep it.” — folk saying collected by Karel Plicka in Slovakia

55.title: Movement of leaflets
55.text: Clover leaflets fold together at night — botanists call this nyctinasty. At night the plant protects itself from cold and damage. Spotting a four-leaf clover after dark is harder because all the leaflets are folded shut.

56.title: MGM logo
56.text: In the 1930s Hollywood studio MGM used a stylised four-leaf clover in its pre-film fanfare as a symbol of misfortune defeated by luck. After a redesign, the lion replaced the clover as the studio's primary mark.

57.title: The Slavic goddess Vesna
57.text: According to Slavic legend, the goddess of spring, Vesna, distributed magical four-leaf clovers across fields and meadows each year as gifts for hard-working people. Whoever found one was rewarded for their diligence and care of the land.

58.title: Most leaflets in Czechia
58.text: The Czech record for the number of separate leaflets of red clover on a single stem is held by an eleven-leaf clover found in 2020 by Lenka Zvědělíková in Libčice nad Vltavou-Letky (that same year collector Jaroslav Kosina also found an eleven-leaf clover in Vyškov).

59.title: Three insights
59.text: Folk wisdom says: who finds a first four-leaf will have luck. Who finds a second will find love. Who finds a third already knows what truly matters in life — and need no longer chase luck or love; they will create them themselves.

60.title: John Steinbeck
60.text: There is no four-leaf clover in John Steinbeck's The Grapes of Wrath — its key symbols are the turtle, Route 66, the grapes and Rose of Sharon's closing gesture. Grandma Joad dies quietly during the night crossing of the desert into California, and Ma hides her death so the family can pass the border inspection.

61.title: Auxin and growth
61.text: Leaf development and leaflet number are governed chiefly by the plant hormone auxin (together with cytokinin), which determine how many leaflets a compound leaf forms. If their balance or transport is disrupted — for instance by mechanical injury or stress — a leaf with an unusual number of leaflets can arise. Such a change is generally not heritable.

62.title: The best time of year
62.text: Most four-leaf clovers are usually found in the warm summer months (June–July), when white clover grows vigorously and long, warm days favour the fourth leaflet. Cooler spring and autumn plants tend to form fewer — lower temperatures and shorter days rather suppress them. The fourth leaflet arises from a combination of genetics and environment (temperature, day length, injury stress, soil conditions), so no season guarantees it.

63.title: World War I
63.text: Soldiers in the First World War trenches gathered four-leaf clovers en masse and sent them home as messages of life. Many family archives across Europe still contain pressed four-leaf clovers from the front, complete with date and location.

64.title: Quiet discovery
64.text: “He bent towards the earth as if to plead with it — and it gave. Four green tears in his palm. Luck, he said softly. Luck, the meadow echoed.” — fragment of a poem by Jiří Orten

65.title: When not to look
65.text: After rain, four-leaf clover hunting is at its hardest — drops of water distort the leaves' shapes and easily fool the eye. Botanists recommend hunting in dry weather mid-morning, when the dew has dried but the sun is not yet beating down directly.

66.title: Welsh flag
66.text: Many people confuse the Welsh leek with the four-leaf clover, but the genuine Celtic luck-charm is distinct from the Welsh national symbol. In Cardiff, the National Museum has a four-leaf-clover collection of more than 4,000 specimens.

67.title: Slavic Veles
67.text: Clover was offered to Veles, the Slavic patron of animals, during spring rituals. According to this tradition the four-leaf clover could call lost livestock back to the herd — and, more generally, bring back anything that had gone astray.

68.title: Vatican collection
68.text: It is sometimes said that four-leaf clovers might be among the small keepsakes held in the Vatican. But no documented records of a separate papal clover collection — nor of a specific 1893 find from the Garden of Gethsemane — have survived; the Garden of Gethsemane is in fact linked more to a folk tale about spotted burclover (Medicago arabica) than to the four-leaf clover.

69.title: Pressed into a Bible
69.text: Christian tradition recommended pressing four-leaf clovers into a Bible — best of all, into Psalm 23 (“The Lord is my shepherd”). The leaf was meant to bind natural luck with spiritual protection. Many family Bibles still hold them.

70.title: Marcel Proust
70.text: Marcel Proust's In Search of Lost Time is famous for its scene of involuntary memory: the taste of a madeleine dipped in tea suddenly summons the narrator's childhood in Combray (bound up with his aunt Léonie). A motif of a pressed four-leaf clover from his grandmother, or “the scent of dried grass” as consolation, does not appear in the work — it is a fabrication.

71.title: Under UV light
71.text: A four-leaf clover is the same plant and the same species as an ordinary trefoil — the fourth leaflet is merely a developmental (genetic) deviation, and both leaf types grow on the same plant. Its chlorophyll is therefore identical, and no special “four-leaf spectrum” exists under UV light. Plants do show chlorophyll fluorescence under ultraviolet light, but that depends on chlorophyll content and leaf condition, not on the number of leaflets, and it is not used to find clovers faster — in practice plain visual searching in good daylight works best.

72.title: A lucky cemetery
72.text: On some old Czech cemeteries it is said that a four-leaf clover found on a loved one's grave is a message from them. Collectors therefore visit old graveyards with respect and often leave a flower in return.

73.title: Charles Lindbergh
73.text: Before his historic transatlantic flight in 1927, Charles Lindbergh received a four-leaf clover for luck from his mother. It crossed the ocean with him and after landing in Paris he pressed it into his diary.

74.title: Dew and four
74.text: “Morning dew, four leaves / a drop in the palm, a quiet breath / which is more, the day or the dream? / who knows, who knows, only the clover knows.” — Šumava folk song, collected by Ladislav Mužík

75.title: Nectar for bees
75.text: The flower of white clover is a major nectar source for bees and bumblebees. Honey from white clover is light and gently sweet. Without clover, Czech beekeeping would lose one of its most important nectar sources.

76.title: Lucky in Japan
76.text: In Japanese culture the four-leaf clover is now a common modern symbol of luck — it appears on stickers, omamori charms and in anime and manga (for example the series “Yotsuba&!” or “Kimi wa Yotsuba no Clover”). The luck symbolism attached to the clover reached Japan with Western culture from about the Meiji period (the latter half of the 19th century), and American influence reinforced it further in the post-war era.

77.title: A druidic ritual
77.text: During the druidic Beltane ritual (1 May) it was the children's task to find a four-leaf clover for the village. The leaf was then hung in the communal hall and protected the inhabitants from disease for the entire year.

78.title: The Kosina herbarium
78.text: The largest documented collection of four- and multi-leaf clovers in Czechia belongs to Jaroslav Kosina of Mikulčice; by the end of 2020 it numbered 16,772 four-leaf clovers (and thousands of other multi-leaf clovers), later over 20,000. Pressed into a herbarium, it was exhibited at the Museum of Records and Curiosities in Pelhřimov. Brno's Mendel Museum does not deal with clovers — its permanent exhibitions are about Gregor Mendel and genetics.

79.title: Throw it over the shoulder
79.text: An old superstition says: if you find a four-leaf clover and want to be rid of bad luck, throw it over your left shoulder without looking back. The bad luck departs with the leaf. If you want to attract luck instead, kiss it and tuck it close to your heart.

81.title: Mutagenic factors
81.text: Four-leaf clovers are not reliably linked to metal contamination of soil. They arise chiefly from the recessive genetic disposition of white clover (Trifolium repens), whose expression is shaped by developmental and environmental factors (temperature, phosphorus fertilisation, the hormone auxin). Claims of a “Leipzig study (2018)” and of iron or zinc as mutagens have no documented scientific source — and iron and zinc are, moreover, essential micronutrients, not recognised mutagens.

82.title: Sixty percent
82.text: Collector statistics show that roughly 60 % of four-leaf clover finds come from places where one has been found before. The genetic predisposition really does cluster locally — it pays off to return to lucky spots.

83.title: Princess Diana
83.text: Princess Diana is said to have had a small four-leaf clover set into her engagement ring — reportedly a gift from her mother. After her death in 1997 the ring with the clover was placed in the Spencer family archives.

84.title: Sibling leaves
84.text: “We were three, we were three — then we became four. We gave three to the earth, the fourth sent up its thanks to heaven.” — anonymous poem from the folk-poetry collection of Wallachia

85.title: Leaflet differentiation
85.text: The leaflets of a clover's compound leaf are initiated at the edge of the shoot apical meristem (SAM) in the early stages of primordium development. Leaflet number is controlled by a gene network described in the model plant Medicago truncatula — for example PALM1, SINGLE LEAFLET1 (SGL1), PINNA1 or the WUS-homolog HEADLESS, whose mutation only occasionally yields an extra leaflet. In white clover (Trifolium repens) four-leaf clovers are attributed to a combination of recessive alleles (mapped to chromosome 4) and environmental influence; a simple “WUSCHEL/CLAVATA pathway defect” as the explanation for the extra leaflet is not documented.

86.title: Renault
86.text: In the 1970s, Renault produced a model 4 whose advertising used the four-leaf clover as its emblem — four wheels, four doors, four leaves of luck. The campaign won an award at the 1972 Cannes advertising festival.

87.title: Aztec fertility
87.text: In Aztec mythology a clover-like leaf was a symbol of the goddess Xochiquetzal, patroness of flowers and fertility. Indigenous people in Mexico still grow a related plant (Trifolium amabile) and believe in its fertility-bringing power.

88.title: Generations of collectors
88.text: Clover collecting is passed down through the generations in some families, and the largest collections take decades to build. The best-known Czech collector is Jaroslav Kosina of Mikulčice, who has gathered over 20,000 four-leaf clovers since 1996 and holds the national record.

89.title: Green for green
89.text: An old folk saying: green dress + four-leaf clover = dazzling love. In the 19th century, brides would pin a four-leaf clover to a green braided sash before the wedding. Today the tradition is occasionally revived at alternative weddings.

90.title: Karel Hynek Mácha
90.text: Karel Hynek Mácha (1810–1836), author of Máj, kept diaries of his travels — in 1834 he journeyed to northern Italy (“Diary of the Journey to Italy”) and had walked to the Giant Mountains as early as 1833; his fiancée was Eleonora “Lori” Šomková. No surviving diary entry of his about finding a four-leaf clover, nor any mention of a “Lorenz”, is documented — so the story of a clover in his breast pocket is given here as an unverified legend, not as a Mácha quotation.

91.title: Foliar anomaly
91.text: A four-leaf clover arises from a rare deviation in leaf development — most often a somatic mutation or a recessive gene variant, when four leaflets are initiated in early leaf development instead of the usual three; environmental factors also play a role. It is no official “type II anomaly”, and there is no single universal principle: “double” flowers (double roses, anemones) arise by a wholly different mechanism — a change in the identity of floral organs (the miR172–APETALA2 pathway, stamens turned into petals) — not by multiplying a leaf structure.

92.title: Collector friends
92.text: There are at least 12 official collector associations for four-leaf clovers worldwide. The largest is the American Four-Leaf Clover Society with more than 3,000 members. In Europe the largest membership belongs to the German Glücksklee-Verein.

93.title: Coronation of Elizabeth II
93.text: At the 1953 coronation of Queen Elizabeth II, her bouquet included a sprig of clover with a four-leaf, given to her by an elderly Scottish woman. After the ceremony the leaf was pressed and kept in the royal archives.

94.title: The day bows down
94.text: “The day bows down like a leaf, / and a four-leaf rises from the grass. / Who would have said, who would have guessed, / that the whole world is in it.” — František Halas, fragment from his 1942 diary

95.title: Forage for cattle
95.text: White clover is now mainly a forage crop. One hectare of clover can support 3–4 cows. For biodynamic farmers it's important to know that four-leaf clovers occur less often in intensively cultivated fields than on wild meadows.

96.title: Coco Chanel
96.text: Coco Chanel kept the four-leaf clover as her personal amulet. After her death, dozens of pressed four-leafs were found in her diaries and books at her villa. Some are now displayed at the Chanel museum in Paris.

97.title: Native American view
97.text: Some North American Indigenous peoples — most notably the Lakota and Cheyenne — venerated the four-leaf clover as a plant connecting the four cardinal directions. A shaman with a four-leaf was said to be able to commune with the powers of all four winds.

98.title: A Zürich collection
98.text: The Swiss bank UBS holds at its Zürich branch a unique collection of 50 pressed four-leaf clovers donated by clients. Each is insured for the symbolic sum of 1 franc and has its own display case bearing the donor's name.

99.title: The Bukovina taboo
99.text: In Bukovina it was once believed that picking a four-leaf clover at midnight invited bad luck on the entire household. Morning dew was fine, the noon sun a blessing, evening — uncertain. But midnight — never.

101.title: A century of observation
101.text: Wageningen University in the Netherlands has been systematically observing the occurrence of four-leaf clovers at selected sites since 1923. The data show a gradual rise in frequency — probably due to climate change and shifts in agriculture.

102.title: Magnetic anomaly
102.text: There is a hypothesis (still unconfirmed) that four-leaf clovers occur more often at sites with a slight geomagnetic anomaly. Collectors with magnetometers report local correlations, but the scientific community accepts this only with caution.

103.title: Apollo 13
103.text: According to some sources, the crew of Apollo 13 carried on board a pressed four-leaf clover given by an astronaut's wife. After the accident and the safe return, it became a relic — today on display at the Kennedy Space Center.

104.title: Park lawn
104.text: “The park lawn, the sun blazing, / a child bends down to look for luck. / In the palm a small four-leaf — / the world pauses for a moment, / it watches and smiles.” — Jiří Suchý, from the collection Quiet Mail

105.title: Selective breeding
105.text: There are ornamental lines of white clover (Trifolium repens) deliberately bred for a higher frequency of four-leaf clovers. The highest proportion arose through gamma-ray irradiation (⁶⁰Co), not classical crossing — the Korean cultivar “Jeju Lucky-1” (around 2010) carries a four-leaf clover in up to ~60% of cases. The ornamental cultivar “Legendary Good Luck” has roughly 50%.

106.title: Walt Disney
106.text: Walt Disney Studios animators have long hidden little jokes and references (so-called “easter eggs”) in their films. The best-known recurring hidden motif, however, is not a four-leaf clover but the “Hidden Mickey” — the silhouette of Mickey's head made from three circles, which fans spot in the background of many films and throughout the Disneyland parks.

107.title: The Chinese view
107.text: In traditional Chinese culture the four-leaf clover has no strong symbolism — the number 4 is unlucky in China because it sounds similar to the word for “death”. Instead, the Chinese prefer the five-leaf symbol, which brings five blessings.

108.title: Speed record
108.text: The most four-leaf clovers found by one person in a single hour is 451 — a 2019 record held by Gabriella Gerhardt of the USA (Guinness World Records).

109.title: Moravian-Slovak borderland
109.text: On the Moravian-Slovak frontier they used to say: “Give the first four-leaf to the one you love. Keep the second. Give the third to whoever needs it most.” This rule influenced many collectors who give four-leaf clovers away to this day.

110.title: Bohumil Hrabal
110.text: In Too Loud a Solitude, Bohumil Hrabal portrays the narrator Haňťa, who for thirty-five years compacts waste paper and rescues rare books he finds in it. There is, however, no mention of four-leaf clovers anywhere in the novel — the recurring green motif is the “green button” of the press, not a clover.

111.title: Four-leaf of luck
111.text:
The four-leaf brings good luck,
its leaflets bring you cheer.
When you find your very first one,
luck will surely lean your way.

112.title: The magical four
112.text: The number 4 holds deep meaning across cultures: four seasons, four cardinal directions, four elements, four gospels. The four-leaf clover is therefore not just a mutation — it is a small embodiment of fourfold balance in nature.

113.title: Eisenhower and the invasion
113.text: According to his aide's memoirs, General Dwight Eisenhower carried in his breast pocket before the Normandy invasion (1944) a pressed four-leaf clover from his wife Mamie. He called it his “little green brother” and it accompanied him through the entire war.

114.title: Morning mist
114.text: “Mist drifts quietly / across an untouched meadow. / Beneath each of its breaths / millions of threes are sleeping. / And one — a single one — four-leaf / waits for me to come.” — Jan Skácel, from the collection Smuténka

115.title: Stress resistance
115.text: A four-leaf clover in white clover results from the interplay of an inherited (recessive) predisposition and external stress: damage to the shoot tip, soil composition or chemicals, temperature swings or drought all raise the chance of a fourth leaflet. A “lucky” four-leaf clover thus often grows on a plant that has faced more stress than its surroundings — though the fourth leaflet is no advantage to the plant, just a developmental deviation.

116.title: Tattoo
116.text: The four-leaf clover is among the most common motifs in modern tattoo art. In the United States it ranks among the top 20 most-tattooed symbols. It is often combined with a horseshoe, a star or the initials of a loved one.

117.title: Hindu tradition
117.text: In Hindu tradition the four-leaf clover is associated with the four Vedas — the ancient spiritual texts. Finding a four-leaf signifies spiritual maturity. In some yogic schools the four-leaf is the symbol of an open heart chakra.

118.title: Astronaut collector
118.text: American astronaut Jim Lovell, commander of the doomed Apollo 13 mission, was also a passionate four-leaf clover collector. After retirement he became an honorary member of several collectors' clubs and donated many of his finds to a NASA museum.

119.title: Many-leaf clover
119.text: Folk wisdom on many-leaf clovers: a five-leaf for money, a six-leaf for fame, a seven-leaf for long life, an eight-leaf for release from debts, a nine-leaf for the luck of the whole family, a ten-leaf for the granting of three wishes.

120.title: F. Scott Fitzgerald
120.text: The name of the heroine Daisy Buchanan in Fitzgerald's The Great Gatsby refers to the daisy flower (its white petals and golden centre symbolising her apparent innocence and hidden corruption), not to a four-leaf clover.

121.title: Population recovery
121.text: After destructive events (fires, mowing) white clover usually re-establishes itself — mainly from the soil seed bank and from surviving roots or rhizomes; the speed of recovery depends on local conditions, and the literature gives no reliable figures. There is no evidence that the frequency of four-leaf clovers is consistently higher in a recovered population; the fourth leaflet is determined chiefly by genetics and temperature, while stress and injury may sometimes raise it slightly.

122.title: Leaf game
122.text: Children in the 19th century used to play a game called “He loves me, he loves me not” with four-leaf clovers — they pulled the leaflets one by one and read the answer from the last. The four-leaf gave the game a dramatic dimension, since the ending was always uncertain.

123.title: Nuremberg trials
123.text: Justice Robert H. Jackson, chief prosecutor at the Nuremberg trials (1945–46), kept according to his memoirs a four-leaf clover from his daughter in his breast pocket. He claimed it kept him calm during the most difficult interrogations.

124.title: When you keep silent
124.text: “When you keep silent and walk through the meadow, / when you don't search and yet you see, / then a four-leaf will give you its hand — / and you would not have seen it for a thousand years.” — Rainer Maria Rilke, free translation from Sonnets to Orpheus

125.title: Seed viability
125.text: White clover seeds are long-lived, but it depends on storage: in dry storage they keep good germination for a matter of years (around 55% after five years), while hard seeds buried in soil can remain viable for even twenty years, albeit with germination of only a few percent (about 1–2%). Collectors who want their own clover patch can buy commercial cultivars with a higher frequency of multi-leaf clovers (e.g. 'Quadrifolium' or 'Good Luck') — the stand fills in roughly in its second year.

126.title: Boeing 747
126.text: Some Boeing 747 crews in the 1980s had a tradition of slipping a four-leaf clover into the logbook before a new aircraft's first flight. The custom was unofficial but widespread — some aircraft were even nicknamed “Lucky”.

127.title: African traditions
127.text: Among some West African peoples (Yoruba, Igbo) a four-leaf is a symbol of ancestral spirits. Shamans use it when communicating with the spirit world. In traditional healing practice the four-leaf is placed inside amulets.

128.title: Twins
128.text: Finding two four-leaf clovers on one plant is not independently rare: the four-leaf mutation clusters on a given plant, so a plant that has already produced one four-leaf clover is more likely to produce another.

129.title: Green kiss
129.text: A Polish folk tradition recommends kissing a four-leaf clover the moment it is found. This is said to “seal a contract” between the plant and the finder — the luck holds as long as the leaf remains in the owner's care. If it is lost or wilts, the contract is broken.

130.title: Jaroslav Seifert
130.text: In 1954 Jaroslav Seifert published the collection “Maminka” (“Mother”) — intimate lyric poetry full of memories of childhood and his mother. The collection contains no poem “Lístky” nor the cited verse about four-leaf clovers; the only “leaves” in the whole book are myrtle leaves in a line from the poem Vějíř. The passage attributed to Seifert about four-leaf clovers is a fabrication.

131.title: Phenotypic plasticity
131.text: Clover is a model plant for the study of phenotypic plasticity — the ability to change appearance with the environment. The same plant can produce only three-leaflets one year and four-leaflets occasionally another. The genome is identical, the expression is not.

132.title: Summer solstice
132.text: The summer solstice is traditionally regarded as the best day to look for four-leaf clovers — the sun's energy is at its peak and anomalies are said to “shine” the most. There is no scientific basis, but many collectors honour this day as their festival.

133.title: Lloyd's of London
133.text: The British insurer Lloyd's of London insured several rare four-leaf clovers for wealthy clients in the 19th century. Insurance file no. 4471 from 1894 describes the insurance of a four-leaf clover for £50 against loss or damage.

134.title: A bouquet of luck
134.text: “I tied a small bouquet from four little clovers, / there were no fives, no sixes — / and yet it was among the loveliest / I have ever tied in my life.” — Jan Werich, fragment from a letter to his granddaughter

135.title: Host fungi
135.text: The roots of white clover live in symbiosis with arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi (e.g. the genus Glomus), which help it take up phosphorus and other elements. Four-leaf clovers are attributed to a combination of recessive genes and external influences (temperature, day length, soil quality, injury or stress); a direct link between abundant mycorrhiza and the frequency of four-leaf clovers is, however, not documented.

136.title: Pelé and football
136.text: Brazilian footballer Pelé kept in his boots before each match a pressed four-leaf clover his mother had given him. The tradition began in 1958 before the World Cup — Pelé scored 6 goals and won the title.

137.title: North American coltsfoot
137.text: Some Indigenous peoples linked the four-leaf clover with a similar plant — coltsfoot. They believed that its four-part leaf was a gift of the Great Spirit to chosen shamans. This tradition blended with European superstition after the cultures met.

138.title: A young record-holder
138.text: The youngest record-holder in finding four-leaf clovers is ten-year-old Katie Borka of Virginia (USA), who in 2018 entered the Guinness Book of Records with a find of 166 four-leaf clovers in a single hour. Guinness does not keep a “per 24 hours” or “per day” category — the timed records are “most in one hour” (451) and “most in eight hours” (887), both held by Gabriella Gerhardt.

139.title: A Slovácko wedding
139.text: There was a tradition in Slovácko that the bride carried a four-leaf clover in her bouquet for luck in the marriage. If she lost it during the ceremony, that was a bad omen. If it stayed in the bouquet, the marriage was to be long and happy.

140.title: Karel Poláček
140.text: In the humorous novel Bylo nás pět (published posthumously in 1946) Karel Poláček tells, in the voice of the schoolboy Petr Bajza, of five boys from a small town and their pranks and superstitions. A specific scene of searching for a lucky four-leaf clover before a geography exam, or a character “Bajaja”, cannot be found in the text, however.

141.title: Stress and regrowth
141.text: Some observers report that four-leaf clovers are a little more common in frequently mown or grazed lawns. But neither a direct effect of mowing nor a “relaxed control of leaflet number” mechanism is scientifically proven — the documented factors behind the anomaly are rather genetics, temperature, day length and hormones. The term “compensatory growth”, moreover, refers in botany to making up lost leaf area after damage, not to a higher incidence of multi-leaf clovers.

142.title: Soft-focus searching
142.text: Experienced searchers advise walking slowly and not focusing the eyes on individual leaflets, but letting the gaze drift freely over the sward — peripheral vision catches a deviation from the regular trefoil pattern better than direct focus. The four-leaf clover then often “pops out” as a square outline among the round trefoils.

143.title: Tonkin and Indochina
143.text: French soldiers in Indochina collected four-leaf clovers in the 1950s and sent them home. An extensive correspondence-based collection grew out of this and is now part of the French war archive. The leaves were perfectly preserved by tropical humidity in the letters.

144.title: Shared luck
144.text: “We divide luck into four / like a clover leaflet, / and the one who receives it whole / forgets that it exists.” — Karel Šiktanc, fragment from the collection Heinovské noci

145.title: Millennial plant
145.text: The root systems of clonal white-clover colonies can persist for thousands of years. Genetic tests show that some colonies in the Scottish highlands are over 5,000 years old — among the oldest organisms in Europe.

146.title: Rolex Datejust
146.text: No documented Rolex model (Datejust included) with a real four-leaf clover set under the crystal, dated 1969, exists — the claim is a fabrication and technically impossible too, because the Datejust only got a sapphire crystal in 1978 (before that it was acrylic/plexi). Watches with a real four-leaf clover embedded in the dial do exist, but they are modern boutique novelties, not a vintage Rolex.

147.title: Tibetan mandalas
147.text: Tibetan mandalas often include a four-leaf motif as a symbol of the four immaterial states of mind — equanimity, compassion, sympathetic joy and calm. In Tibetan art the four-leaf is the green ray of awakening.

148.title: Collective collection
148.text: The Austrian village of Klee-Heim has run a collective collection since 1965, into which residents can donate their finds. The collection now holds more than 80,000 four-leaf clovers with the finders' signatures and dates. It is exhibited in the local town hall.

149.title: Three returns
149.text: An old superstition: if you find a four-leaf clover and want the luck to be threefold, you must return to the find spot three times in the same week. Each time, thank the meadow. Whoever does so will reportedly have “triple-base luck” — health, love and peace.

151.title: CRISPR-Cas9
151.text: In 2022 researchers used CRISPR-Cas9 to edit a gene in white clover so that it produced four-leaf clovers reliably. Commercial use is still marginal — hand-found wild leaves carry more symbolic value than genetically engineered ones.

152.title: Thirteen-leaf
152.text: A thirteen-leaf is extremely rare and in some cultures paradoxically brings bad luck — owing to the superstition surrounding the number 13. Many collectors give thirteen-leaf clovers away to rid themselves of the “excess of luck” that might tip into the opposite.

153.title: Munich Olympics 1972
153.text: The mascot of the 1972 Munich Olympics was the dachshund Waldi in the Olympic colours. Among the souvenirs, however, the four-leaf clover also appeared — as a symbol of Bavarian local luck. More than two million souvenir items were sold.

154.title: Without end
154.text: “A four-leaf without end is every moment / in which I meet you. / They grow around us, / we just don't see them — / until we bend down / to the earth, to you, to ourselves.” — Vladimír Mikeš, fragment from the collection The Gingerbread Wagon

155.title: Striated leaves
155.text: The Italian racing team Alfa Romeo has carried the quadrifoglio — a green four-leaf clover — in its emblem since 1923. The symbol was first used by racer Ugo Sivocci, who won the Targa Florio in Sicily on 15 April 1923. Sivocci died five months later at Monza in a car WITHOUT the clover — which is why a white square replaced the original triangle.

156.title: The Alfa Romeo badge
156.text: The Italian motor-racing team Alfa Romeo carries the quadrifoglio — a green four-leaf clover — on its badge. The driver Ugo Sivocci had it painted on his car, originally as a clover in a white square, and won the 1923 Targa Florio with it. When Sivocci was killed that same year in a crash at Monza — in a car that lacked the clover — the symbol became the team's permanent emblem from the 1924 season. In his memory the white square was replaced by a triangle; the missing corner recalls the lost driver.

157.title: Gabriella Gerhardt
157.text: American Gabriella Gerhardt holds five Guinness World Records: most four-leaf clovers in an hour (451 in 2019), in 8 hours (887 in 2018), the largest collection of six-leaf (1,437) and seven-leaf (209) clovers, and the largest overall four-leaf collection — 118,791 pieces, surpassing Edward Martin's long-standing record in 2023.

158.title: Edward Martin
158.text: Before Gabriella Gerhardt, the record for the largest collection of four-leaf clovers was held by American Edward Martin — 111,060 pieces. For years his collection was considered the largest in the world (the record was verified around 2007), until Gerhardt surpassed it in 2023 with 118,791 four-leaf clovers.

159.title: Frankie Osborn
159.text: Real-estate agent Frankie Osborn from Shelby County, Alabama, has roughly 70,000 four-leaf and many-leaf clovers in her collection. Among them are one nine-leaf and two eight-leaf clovers. Most of her finds come from the area around her home in Westover.

160.title: Suzi Mekhitarian
160.text: In 2014, Australian Suzi Mekhitarian found a contiguous patch in her front garden with 21 four-leaf clovers. The probability of such an occurrence under normal conditions would be astronomical — confirming that the genetics of a clonal colony plays a huge role.

161.title: Watanabe's story
161.text: Yoshiharu Watanabe, holder of the 63-leaflet record, combined natural and manual cross-breeding. In 2020 he had a clover with 49 leaflets, in 2021 with 55 — and in August 2023 he unexpectedly discovered a plant he thought had 56 leaflets. After recounting, he realised it was 63.

162.title: BRIT herbarium
162.text: The Botanical Research Institute of Texas (BRIT) has 556 white-clover sheets in its herbarium, gathered over more than 150 years. An internal survey showed that only 6 of them (1.08 %) contain a four-leaf clover — a record that mirrors the natural rarity of the anomaly.

163.title: Stolons and colonies
163.text: White clover spreads through stolons — creeping above-ground runners. New roots and leaves grow from the nodes of the stolons. The whole colony is a clone — a genetically identical copy of the mother plant. If it has a genetic predisposition for anomalies, every plant in the colony shares it.

164.title: Lifespan
164.text: The original (mother) white-clover plant and its root usually die within one to two years. But the clump constantly renews itself by vegetative growth from stolons and from the soil seed bank, and as a clone (genet) it can live for decades, exceptionally even over a hundred years. A four-leaf clover found this year may thus be genetically identical or related to last year's.

165.title: Nitrogen fixation
165.text: The nodules on clover roots contain bacteria of the species Rhizobium leguminosarum, which convert atmospheric nitrogen into ammonium ions. This process is called biological nitrogen fixation and is the reason clover improves the soil for the plants around it.

166.title: Allopolyploidy
166.text: White clover arose by allopolyploidy — the crossing of two different species (the alpine T. pallescens and the Atlantic-coastal T. occidentale) in western Europe during the last ice age, roughly 15,000 to 28,000 years ago, when the ranges of the two species overlapped in glacial refugia. The resulting organism has two complete chromosome sets from different parents, 2n = 4x = 32 in total.

167.title: Compensatory growth
167.text: After mowing, clover makes up its lost biomass by accelerated regrowth of new leaves (compensatory growth) — a documented phenomenon. That this accelerated growth would “relax” the control of leaflet number and raise the incidence of four-leaf clovers is not reliably documented; the known factors affecting leaflet number are genetics (recessive alleles, transposons and epigenetics) and modulators such as auxin, phosphates, warmth and light. Experienced collectors add that four-leaf clovers are easiest to find in spring before the first mowing, when the stand is uncluttered.

168.title: Plasticity of expression
168.text: White clover is a favourite model plant for studying phenotypic plasticity — the ability to change appearance according to the environment (light, nutrients, temperature). Whether a given individual forms a four-leaf clover is not purely a matter of environment, though: there must be a genetic basis (a recessive allele, a somatic mutation, epigenetics), and only then do external conditions decide whether the fourth leaflet actually appears. The individual's genome stays the same; the expression of the trait depends on the combination of genes and environment.

169.title: Medicago truncatula
169.text: For research into leaf development and anomalies, the related Medicago truncatula is often used — a close relative of white clover. Its genome was sequenced earlier and serves as a reference model for studying the genetics of legumes, including the mechanisms behind four-leaf formation.

170.title: Mendel's legacy
170.text: Gregor Mendel ran his famous experiments with the garden pea (Pisum sativum) — a legume related to clover. The pea is diploid and self-pollinating, which let Mendel grow pure lines and cross them easily. With white clover (Trifolium repens) he would have hit two obstacles: it is an allotetraploid (2n=4x=32) and is cross-pollinating (self-sterile), so it could be neither reliably self-pollinated nor kept in pure lines — it was precisely the pea's simplicity that let Mendel uncover the basic laws of heredity.

171.title: Meadow biodiversity
171.text: White clover is an important meadow plant — it attracts pollinators and, as a legume, enriches the soil with nitrogen. Its effect on species diversity varies, though: in managed swards it can support diversity, whereas in natural and semi-natural meadows its overabundance (and the nitrogen it supplies) can instead reduce it. In this light a four-leaf clover is a small expression of nature's variety.

172.title: Naval tradition
172.text: The four-leaf clover has been a widely known symbol of luck since the 17th century and is not missing from sailors' varied talismans and superstitions. A specific tradition of the four-leaf clover in the 18th- and 19th-century British navy, pressed clovers in captains' and officers' diaries, or their keeping in the collections of naval museums in Greenwich and Portsmouth, could not, however, be documented in any credible source.

173.title: Victorian herbaria
173.text: In Victorian England, pressing plants — including four-leaf clovers — was a popular pastime among ladies of the upper classes. Special albums of dated finds became fashionable. Many 19th-century family archives still contain carefully kept specimens with notes.

174.title: Soldiers at the front
174.text: In both world wars soldiers carried four-leaf clovers as lucky charms and enclosed pressed plants and flowers in their letters home — a message of hope and proof that they were alive and thinking of home. Much such correspondence has survived in museum and family archives.

175.title: Quatrefoil in the Gothic
175.text: In Gothic architecture the four-leaf rosette (quatrefoil) is a common ornament on windows and friezes. Geometrically it corresponds to the four-leaf clover — four circular arcs inscribed in a square. Famous examples are the quatrefoils on the baptistery of San Giovanni in Florence.

176.title: Van Cleef Alhambra
176.text: Van Cleef & Arpels' Alhambra collection of jewellery is inspired by the four-leaf clover and the Moorish quatrefoil. The first necklace with 20 four-leaf motifs was launched in 1968. The line is still one of the best-selling luxury jewellery collections in the world.

177.title: Čtyřlístek on stamps
177.text: Between 2010 and 2011 the Czech Post issued a series of four postage stamps with characters from the Čtyřlístek comic; Fifinka was the first onto a stamp in late April 2010, Bobík the last in May 2011. The artwork was created by the comic's author, Jaroslav Němeček. The issue marked over 40 years of Čtyřlístek (the comic first appeared on 15 May 1969); a separate anniversary series followed only for the 50th anniversary in 2019.

178.title: Quadrifoglio in F1
178.text: From 2018 to 2023 the four-leaf clover (quadrifoglio) appeared on the cars of the team racing under the Alfa Romeo name (in 2018 as “Alfa Romeo Sauber”, from 2019 as “Alfa Romeo Racing” and “Alfa Romeo F1 Team”), run by the Sauber stable. Already in 2017 it was carried by the Ferrari SF70H and on the overalls of Ferrari drivers Vettel and Räikkönen — as a tribute to the historic bond between Alfa Romeo and Ferrari.

179.title: Field dew
179.text: In some Moravian villages it was believed that only a four-leaf clover bearing morning dew has any real power. A dry leaf was just a decoration. Traditional collectors therefore got up at dawn and gathered them in the very earliest hours of the day.

180.title: Dry it head down
180.text: The folk way of pressing a four-leaf clover called for turning the leaf so that the stem pointed up and the leaflets pointed down. This way, it was said, the luck would not run out of the plant. The ritual had no botanical basis, but for many families it was symbolically important.

181.title: Write a wish
181.text: A folk charm advised writing a secret wish in ink on a four-leaf clover and tucking the leaf into a book. If the leaf stayed green for at least a year, the wish would be granted. If it turned brown, the wish was rejected and the seeker had to start again with a new find.

182.title: A mutual gift
182.text: In some regions of Bohemia and Moravia it was customary to give a four-leaf clover exactly as one had found it — root, soil and all. A dried leaf, it was said, lost its power; only a fresh one was a true gift of luck from the finder to the recipient.

183.title: Pressing the find
183.text: Classic pressing of a four-leaf clover takes place between sheets of absorbent paper under a weight for 7 to 14 days. Humidity control is important — too damp a leaf moulds, too dry a leaf shatters. Replacing the paper every 2 to 3 days ensures even drying.

184.title: Pressed colour
184.text: A properly pressed four-leaf clover keeps its green colour much longer if protected from direct light — usually for years, exceptionally for decades in acid-free, UV-protected storage. Over time it shifts to yellow-green and brown-green as the chlorophyll breaks down. Collectors therefore keep their finds in dark albums or special boxes.

185.title: Photographic record
185.text: Modern collectors often photograph a find right on the spot — ideally in the meadow itself (in situ). The photograph captures the surrounding plants and the site's conditions. Adding a pressed (herbarium) specimen to the photograph is one of the tried-and-tested ways of documentation, as used in botanical practice too.

186.title: Citizen science
186.text: Some clover projects have involved the public in so-called citizen science — people photograph and report their finds through apps such as iNaturalist. A systematic scientific analysis of the frequency of this anomaly across regions, altitudes and climates from such data barely exists yet, though; the current frequency estimates (around 1:5,000) come rather from targeted surveys than from citizen science.

187.title: Botanical classification
187.text: In botany the four-leaf anomaly is classed among so-called teratological changes — deviations from the typical phenotype. The multiplication of leaflets in a clover's compound leaf is termed pleiophylly. Such anomalies have been recorded systematically in the specialist literature since the 19th century (e.g. Masters, Vegetable Teratology, 1869).

188.title: The tree and four roots
188.text: In a European folk tale of the world's creation, four great roots beneath the earth hold all continents in place. Each leaflet of a four-leaf clover symbolises one of these roots — to find a four-leaf clover therefore means to connect with the primordial forces that hold the world together.

189.title: The chosen finder
189.text: In many European folk traditions the finder of a four-leaf clover is regarded as chosen — not merely a lucky person, but someone capable of seeing hidden things. The four-leaf, it is said, cannot be hunted with selfish intent; the plant only reveals itself to those with a pure heart.

190.title: Shamanic amulets
190.text: In the traditional shamanic healing of various cultures, four-part leaves serve as a component of protective amulets. The principle is universal — four-fold symmetry evokes the balance of the four elements and offers magical protection to the bearer.

191.title: Folklore collections
191.text: In the 19th century ethnographers began systematically collecting folk oral tradition — a landmark is Sušil's collection Moravské národní písně (Moravian Folk Songs, published between 1835 and 1860). The collecting and documenting of folk culture continued into the 20th century, for example in the work of the folklorist and photographer Karel Plicka (Český rok). Folk songs and rhymes feature the motif of clover and searching in a meadow, with which the four-leaf clover as a symbol of luck is traditionally associated.

192.title: Fairy-tale motifs
192.text: In European folk narrative the four-leaf clover appears as a magic talisman — said to reveal deception and let one see the hidden (in the Brothers Grimm tale “The Cock's Beam” a girl sees through a magician's illusion thanks to a clover) and to protect against evil forces. The motif is most strongly documented in the English and German tradition; in classic Czech fairy tales it appears as a fixed motif only marginally.

193.title: Romantic poetry
193.text: The Romantic poetry of the 19th century took to the four-leaf clover as a symbol of nature, mystery and personal destiny. Poets used it as a metaphor for rare moments of luck — an image that has survived in modern poetry and pop culture alike.

194.title: Contemporary literature
194.text: In Czech culture the four-leaf clover tends to be associated with luck and with nostalgia for childhood, when people searched for clovers in meadows and believed in nature's simple magic. Nostalgia for childhood is itself a common motif in Czech lyric poetry (e.g. in Jaroslav Seifert), though the specific role of the four-leaf clover as a symbol in contemporary literature is not established in literary scholarship.

195.title: The path
195.text: Walk a quiet meadow, do not ask, do not measure your steps. The four-leaf does not seek the one who counts — it finds the one who pauses. Anonymous folk verse.

196.title: Luck in your hand
196.text: What the palm holds, the palm will lose. What the heart hides, the heart carries. A four-leaf in the palm — a dream for a moment. A four-leaf in the heart — a whole life. Folk proverb.

197.title: Beneath the blue sky
197.text: Beneath the blue sky a green meadow, on the meadow thick clover blooms. Find four leaves, a small piece of luck — and walk on, greater luck awaits you. Anonymous folk rhyme.

198.title: Verses from the countryside
198.text: Fields and forests, clover blooms, four leaves in a dense little world. Whoever finds them is at home — their house is the whole earth. A folk poem from central Bohemia.

199.title: A turn of the hand
199.text: Whoever believes will bend down. Whoever doesn't search will find. Whoever gives away will hold. So the four-leaf teaches. Folk wisdom in verse.

200.title: Luck for three
200.text: Three leaves for me, three for you, the fourth belongs to whoever meets us. Luck divided is luck doubled. Folk proverb from Moravia.

201.title: Fractal likeness
201.text: The venation of many plants' leaves branches dendritically — the main vein divides into ever finer veins, and this pattern shows a certain self-similarity, which mathematicians describe in natural structures using the so-called fractal dimension. It is not a perfect fractal, though, and a four-leaf clover has no special “fractal symmetry” beyond an ordinary trefoil; a clover leaf has ordinary pinnate (reticulate) venation. The term “fractal isomorphism” is not an established botanical concept.

202.title: Sun vs. shade
202.text: The sun is a friend to clover, but shade is a friend to the four-leaf hunter.

203.title: Five minutes and on
203.text: Don't search for more than 5 minutes — better to move on to another spot. Four-leaf clovers don't grow everywhere.

205.title: Better not at night
205.text: Even with a headlamp you won't find many at night — the four-leaf “folds up” for the night.

206.title: I'll send you a four-leaf
206.text: Never found a four-leaf? Doesn't matter, more left for me :-)) But if you'd like one, get in touch and I'll happily find one for you and send it your way :-)) FREE OF CHARGE !!!

207.title: Scythes and mowers
207.text: You know what the worst pest is for four-leaf clovers? Scythes and lawnmowers...

208.title: Rarer than a four-leaf
208.text: Want to know what's rarer than a four-leaf and the other many-leafs? An unfaked two-leaf!!! :))

209.title: Once even a 10-leaf
209.text: I only collect four-leaf clovers, but once I plucked a 10-leaf as well.

210.title: Benefice 2025
210.text: A given four-leaf clover brings luck to the recipient, not to the picker. That is why in 2025 the author, as part of a charity drive, sold four-leaf clovers for over 10,000 CZK. Thanks to all the contributors!!!

666.title: A mysterious find
666.text: Not every four-leaf clover seen on a find photograph is one I actually found and picked :-((( Find no. 666 was never properly photographed!!! Coincidence? :)
